category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Complex Analysis/Applications and Advanced Topics

info.text: CSIR NET level MSQ questions on Complex Analysis — advanced topics: harmonic analysis, Hilbert transform, reflection principle, analytic number theory connections, Riemann surfaces.

name.text: CA30-Q01 Analytic Functions — Higher-Order Cauchy Formula

questiontext.text: Using the Cauchy integral formula for derivatives, evaluate \(\displaystyle I = \oint_{|z|=2}\dfrac{z^3+2z}{(z-1)^4}\,dz\):

feedback.text: With \(f(z)=z^3+2z\) and pole of order 4 at \(z_0=1\): \(I = \frac{2\pi i}{3!}f'''(1)\). \(f'''(z) = 6\), so \(f'''(1)=6\). Thus \(I = \frac{2\pi i}{6}\times6 = 2\pi i\). Let me re-check: \(\oint\frac{f(z)}{(z-z_0)^{n+1}}dz = \frac{2\pi i}{n!}f^{(n)}(z_0)\). Here \(n=3\): \(I=\frac{2\pi i}{3!}f'''(1) = \frac{2\pi i}{6}\cdot6 = 2\pi i\). The correct answer is \(2\pi i\).
\(f(z)=z^3+2z\). \(f'''(z)=6\), so \(f'''(1)=6\). By the formula: \(\oint\frac{f(z)}{(z-1)^4}dz = \frac{2\pi i}{3!}f'''(1) = \frac{2\pi i}{6}\cdot6 = 2\pi i\). This is the correct answer.
The formula gives \(2\pi i\cdot f'''(1)/3! = 2\pi i\). Not \(4\pi i\).
There is a pole of order 4 at \(z=1\) inside \(|z|=2\), so the integral is non-zero. It equals \(2\pi i\).

questiontext.text: The Borel-Carathéodory theorem: if \(f\) is analytic in \(|z|\leq R\) and \(M(r) = \max_{|z|=r}|f(z)|\), \(A(r) = \max_{|z|=r}\text{Re}(f(z))\), then \(M(r) \leq \dfrac{2r}{R-r}A(R) + \dfrac{R+r}{R-r}|f(0)|\). This shows the maximum modulus is controlled by:

answer.text: The maximum of the real part on a larger circle

feedback.text: The theorem controls \(|f|\) on the disk of radius \(r\) by \(\max\text{Re}(f)\) on the larger disk of radius \(R>r\). This is a quantitative version of the fact that real parts of analytic functions are harmonic (hence bounded by boundary values) and provides estimates for growth of analytic functions.

answer.text: The maximum imaginary part on the same circle

feedback.text: The theorem uses \(\text{Re}(f)\) on a LARGER circle (radius \(R>r\)), not Im\((f)\) on the same circle.

answer.text: The number of zeros of \(f\) inside the disk

feedback.text: Counting zeros gives Jensen's formula, not the Borel-Carathéodory estimate. The B-C theorem involves real parts.

answer.text: The value of \(f\) at the center only

feedback.text: The theorem involves the maximum of \(\text{Re}(f)\) on the boundary circle of radius \(R\), plus the central value. The boundary term dominates for large \(A(R)\).

name.text: CA30-Q03 Conformal Mapping and Heat Flow

questiontext.text: In steady-state heat flow, the temperature \(T\) satisfies \(\Delta T = 0\) (Laplace equation). If a conformal map \(w=f(z)\) maps domain \(D\) to \(\Omega\), and \(T\) is harmonic in \(\Omega\), then \(T\circ f\) is harmonic in \(D\) because:

answer.text: The composition of a harmonic function with a conformal map is harmonic (harmonicity is preserved under conformal mappings)

feedback.text: If \(T\) is harmonic and \(f\) is analytic, then \(T\circ f\) is harmonic. This follows because \(T=\text{Re}(H)\) for some analytic \(H\), and \(H\circ f\) is also analytic, so \(\text{Re}(H\circ f) = T\circ f\) is harmonic. This is the fundamental principle enabling conformal mapping to solve boundary value problems.

answer.text: Conformal maps preserve the Laplacian operator exactly: \(\Delta(T\circ f)=(\Delta T)\circ f\)

feedback.text: This is NOT true in general. The Laplacian transforms by \(\Delta(T\circ f) = |f'|^2 (\Delta T)\circ f\), which is zero when \(\Delta T=0\), but the operators are not equal. Harmonicity (being in the kernel of \(\Delta\)) IS preserved.

answer.text:
The chain rule gives \(
abla(T\circ f) = 
abla T \cdot 
abla f\), which is zero when \(
abla T=0\)

feedback.text: This confuses the gradient (for isothermal curves) with harmonicity. Harmonicity means \(\Delta T=0\), not \(\nabla T=0\).

answer.text: Conformal maps preserve all differential equations, not just Laplace's equation

feedback.text: Conformal maps preserve the FORM of Laplace's equation (due to preservation of angles), but not all differential equations. For example, the wave equation is not conformally invariant in general.

questiontext.text: The Prime Number Theorem \(\pi(x)\sim x/\ln x\) (where \(\pi(x)\) counts primes \(\leq x\)) was proved using complex analysis: specifically, showing \(\zeta(s)\neq0\) on the line \(\text{Re}(s)=1\). The key complex-analytic step is:

answer.text: The non-vanishing of \(\zeta(1+it)\) for real \(t\neq0\) allows Perron's formula to give an asymptotic for \(\pi(x)\)

feedback.text: The Hadamard-de la Vallée Poussin proof uses: (1) The Euler product shows \(\log\zeta(s)\) encodes primes. (2) The non-vanishing \(\zeta(1+it)\neq0\) (proved using \(3\cos\theta+4\cos2\theta+\cos3\theta\geq0\)) prevents the boundary contribution from Perron's contour. (3) This gives \(\psi(x)\sim x\) (Chebyshev function), equivalent to PNT.

answer.text: The Cauchy integral formula applied to the counting function \(\pi(x)\)

feedback.text: The Cauchy integral formula is not directly applied to \(\pi(x)\) (a step function). Perron's formula converts the sum into a contour integral of \(\zeta'/\zeta\).

answer.text: The Riemann Hypothesis: all zeros of \(\zeta\) lie on \(\text{Re}(s)=1/2\)

feedback.text: The Riemann Hypothesis implies stronger error terms but is NOT needed for the basic PNT. The standard proof only needs \(\zeta(1+it)\neq0\) (on the boundary of the critical strip, not inside).

answer.text: The functional equation of \(\zeta\) relating \(\zeta(s)\) to \(\zeta(1-s)\)

feedback.text: The functional equation is used to extend \(\zeta\) to the whole plane, but the core of the PNT proof is the non-vanishing on \(\text{Re}(s)=1\).

questiontext.text: The Riemann surface of \(w = \sqrt{z(z-1)(z-2)}\) (a hyperelliptic curve of genus 1) has branch points at \(z=0,1,2,\infty\). The genus of a compact Riemann surface associated with \(w^2 = (z-a_1)(z-a_2)\cdots(z-a_{2g+2})\) (with \(2g+2\) branch points) is:

feedback.text: By the Riemann-Hurwitz formula: for a 2-sheeted cover of \(\hat{\mathbb{C}}\) with \(2g+2\) branch points (each with ramification 2), \(\chi(\text{surface}) = 2\chi(\hat{\mathbb{C}}) - (2g+2)\cdot1 = 4-(2g+2)=2-2g\). So genus \(= g\). For \(w^2=z(z-1)(z-2)\): \(2g+2=4\) (including \(\infty\)), giving \(g=1\) (elliptic curve/torus).
The number \(2g+2\) is the number of branch points, not the genus. The genus is \(g\), determined by the Riemann-Hurwitz formula.
The Riemann-Hurwitz formula gives genus \(g\), not \(g+1\). For 4 branch points (\(g=1\)), the genus is 1 (torus), not 2.
With \(2g+2\geq4\) branch points, the genus \(g\geq1\), not 0. Genus 0 corresponds to only 2 branch points (then the cover is just another sphere).

questiontext.text: The Hilbert transform of a function \(u(x)\) is \((\mathcal{H}u)(x) = \text{P.V.}\dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^\infty\dfrac{u(t)}{x-t}dt\). For a function \(f=u+iv\) analytic in the upper half-plane with \(u = \text{Re}(f)\) on the real axis, the imaginary part \(v = \text{Im}(f)\) is related to \(u\) by:

feedback.text: The boundary values of an analytic function in the upper half-plane satisfy the Hilbert transform relation: \(v = \mathcal{H}u\) (given by the Plemelj-Sokhotski formula). This is the real-line version of the Cauchy integral formula. The Hilbert transform is the imaginary part of the analytic signal.

answer.text: \(v = u\) (real and imaginary parts are equal)

feedback.text: This is only true for very special functions. In general, \(v=\mathcal{H}u\) (the Hilbert transform), not \(v=u\).
This would hold only if \(\mathcal{H}u = -u\), which happens for specific functions (e.g., \(u=\sin x\), \(v=-\cos x\)). In general \(v=\mathcal{H}u\).
The CR equations strongly constrain \(v\) once \(u\) is known (up to an additive constant). On the real line, this constraint is exactly the Hilbert transform relation.

questiontext.text: Vitali's convergence theorem: if \(\{f_n\}\) is a locally bounded sequence of analytic functions in a domain \(D\), and \(f_n\to f\) pointwise on a set \(E\subset D\) with a limit point in \(D\), then \(f_n\to f\) locally uniformly in \(D\). This refines Montel's theorem. An immediate corollary:

answer.text: A locally bounded sequence converging on a set with a limit point converges everywhere in the domain

feedback.text: Vitali's theorem says: if the sequence is locally bounded (thus normal by Montel) AND converges on a set with a limit point, then every subsequential limit must agree on \(E\) (by Identity Theorem), so the entire sequence converges to the same limit everywhere — locally uniformly.

answer.text: Every locally bounded sequence of analytic functions converges

feedback.text: Without convergence on a set with a limit point, a locally bounded sequence might just be normal (have convergent subsequences) without the full sequence converging.

answer.text: Pointwise convergence implies uniform convergence on the whole domain

feedback.text: Vitali gives locally uniform convergence (uniform on compact subsets), not globally uniform convergence on the possibly unbounded domain \(D\).

answer.text: Every subsequential limit of \(\{f_n\}\) equals \(f\) everywhere in \(D\)

feedback.text: This is actually a direct consequence of the Vitali theorem (since any limit \(g\) agrees with \(f\) on \(E\), hence \(g\equiv f\) by the Identity Theorem). This is the conclusion, making it correct.

questiontext.text: The Monodromy Theorem: if a function element \((f, D)\) can be analytically continued along every path in a simply connected domain \(\Omega\), then the continuation defines a single-valued analytic function in \(\Omega\). This is used to conclude:

answer.text: \(\log z\) and \(\sqrt{z}\) are multi-valued on \(\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}\) because \(\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}\) is not simply connected

feedback.text: On the simply connected domain \(\mathbb{C}\setminus(-\infty,0]\), both \(\log z\) and \(\sqrt{z}\) have single-valued branches. On the non-simply-connected \(\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}\), continuation around the origin produces different branches. The Monodromy Theorem explains why simple connectivity is needed for single-valuedness.

answer.text: Every function analytic in a disk extends to the whole plane

feedback.text: This is false; e.g., \(f(z)=1/(z-2)\) is analytic in \(|z|<1\) but has a pole at \(z=2\). The Monodromy Theorem is about simply connected domains, not the whole plane.

answer.text: Multi-valued functions don't exist in complex analysis

feedback.text: Multi-valued functions (like \(\log z\)) exist; the Monodromy Theorem gives conditions under which single-valued branches can be chosen.

answer.text: Analytic continuation always gives a single-valued result

feedback.text: Analytic continuation in non-simply-connected domains can be multi-valued. The Monodromy Theorem requires simple connectivity to guarantee single-valuedness.

name.text: CA30-Q09 Entire Functions — Picard's Theorem Application

questiontext.text: By Picard's Great Theorem, in every punctured neighborhood of an essential singularity, a function takes every complex value (with at most one exception) infinitely often. For \(f(z) = \sin(1/z)\), determine which value is taken only finitely many times near \(z=0\):

answer.text: No value — all values are taken infinitely often (no exceptional value for \(\sin(1/z)\))

feedback.text: \(\sin(1/z)\) has an essential singularity at 0. By Picard's theorem, every value is taken infinitely often in any punctured neighborhood, with at most one exception. For \(\sin w\), no complex value is omitted (unlike \(e^w\) which omits 0). So \(\sin(1/z)\) has no exceptional value: every \(w\in\mathbb{C}\) is taken infinitely often.

answer.text: The value 0 (since \(\sin(1/z)=0\) requires \(1/z=n\pi\), giving only countably many zeros)

feedback.text: The value 0 IS taken infinitely often (at \(z=1/(n\pi)\) for all integers \(n\neq0\)). "Countably many" is still infinitely many, so 0 is not exceptional.

answer.text: The value \(\pm1\) (the extreme values of the real sine function)

feedback.text: \(\sin(1/z)=1\) when \(1/z=\pi/2+2k\pi\), giving \(z=2/((\pi+4k\pi))\) — infinitely many solutions near 0. Both \(\pm1\) are taken infinitely often.

For real arguments, \(|\sin x|\leq1\), but for complex \(z\), \(\sin(z)\) can take any complex value. Near the essential singularity at 0, \(\sin(1/z)\) takes all values including those with large modulus.

questiontext.text: To evaluate \(\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty f(n)\), integrate \(\pi\cot(\pi z)f(z)\) over a large rectangle, using the residues at integers (where \(\text{Res}(\pi\cot(\pi z);n)=1\)). This gives \(\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty f(n) = -\sum[\text{Res of }\pi\cot(\pi z)f(z)\text{ at non-integer poles}]\). For \(f(z)=1/(z^2+a^2)\) with \(a>0\), the result is:

feedback.text: The poles of \(\pi\cot(\pi z)/(z^2+a^2)\) at \(z=\pm ia\): \(\text{Res}(f;ia) = \pi\cot(\pi ia)/(2ia) = \pi i\coth(\pi a)/(2ia) = \pi\coth(\pi a)/(2a)\). Similarly at \(-ia\). Total: \(-(\pi\coth(\pi a)/(2a)+\pi\coth(\pi a)/(2a)) = -\pi\coth(\pi a)/a\). By the formula: \(\sum f(n) = -(-\pi\coth(\pi a)/a) = \pi\coth(\pi a)/a\).
The series \(\sum 1/(n^2+a^2)\) evaluates to \(\pi\coth(\pi a)/a\), not \(\pi/(a\sinh(\pi a))\).
This equals \(\pi\coth(\pi a)/a\) only if \(\coth=1/\tanh\). Actually \(\coth(\pi a)=1/\tanh(\pi a)\), so \(\pi\coth(\pi a)/a = \pi/(a\tanh(\pi a))\). These ARE equal. So this is also correct.
This would be the value of \(\sum 1/n^2 \cdot 1/a^2 = \pi^2/(6a^2)\) (by Basel), which is different from \(\sum 1/(n^2+a^2)\).

questiontext.text: The analytic capacity of a compact set \(K\subset\mathbb{C}\) is \(\gamma(K) = \sup|f'(\infty)|\) where the sup is over all analytic \(f:\mathbb{C}\setminus K\to\mathbb{D}\) with \(f(\infty)=0\), and \(f'(\infty)=\lim_{z\to\infty}zf(z)\). The Painlevé problem asks: which compact sets are "removable" for bounded analytic functions? The answer (Tolsa, 2003) is related to:

answer.text: Geometric measure theory: \(K\) is removable iff it has zero 1-dimensional Hausdorff measure

feedback.text: The Painlevé problem: \(K\) is removable for bounded analytic functions iff \(\gamma(K)=0\). Tolsa proved \(\gamma(K)=0\) iff the 1-dimensional Menger curvature integral of \(K\) is zero, which relates to \(K\) having zero length in a suitable sense. For "rectifiable" sets, removability corresponds to zero 1-d Hausdorff measure.

answer.text: Topology: \(K\) is removable iff it is not connected

feedback.text: Connectivity is not the relevant condition. Removability is measured by analytic capacity, which is related to geometric measure theory properties of \(K\).

answer.text: Algebra: \(K\) is removable iff it consists of finitely many points

feedback.text: Single points are removable by Riemann's theorem. But \(K\) can be infinite (e.g., a Cantor set with zero Hausdorff measure) and still be removable.

answer.text: Functional analysis: \(K\) is removable iff every bounded analytic function extends continuously to \(K\)

feedback.text: Removability means every bounded analytic function on \(\mathbb{C}\setminus K\) extends to a constant (has trivial bounded analytic structure). Continuous extension is a different (stronger) condition.

name.text: CA30-Q12 Dirichlet Problem via Conformal Mapping

questiontext.text: The Dirichlet problem: find \(u\) harmonic in domain \(D\) with prescribed boundary values \(u=g\) on \(\partial D\). For the upper half-plane \(\mathbb{H}\) with boundary data \(g(t)\) on the real line, the solution is the Poisson integral \(u(x,y) = \dfrac{y}{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^\infty\dfrac{g(t)}{(x-t)^2+y^2}dt\). For the boundary data \(g(t) = \begin{cases}1 & t>0 \\ 0 & t<0\end{cases}\), the harmonic function is:

feedback.text: For the step function boundary data: \(u = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{\pi}\arctan(x/y) = \frac{1}{\pi}\arg(z)\) where \(z=x+iy\). This equals 1 when the argument \(\to0\) (positive real axis) and 0 when argument \(\to\pi\) (negative real axis). Alternatively, \(u = \frac{1}{\pi}\text{Arg}(z)\) is harmonic in \(\mathbb{H}\) with the correct boundary values.
This is harmonic (the imaginary part of \(-1/z\)) but doesn't match the boundary conditions. At \(y\to0\): \(u\to0\) for all \(x\neq0\), not the step function.
This is harmonic (the real part of \(e^{iz}\)) but doesn't satisfy the boundary conditions: at \(y=0\), \(u(x,0)=\cos x\), not a step function.
This is harmonic but at \(y\to0\): \(u\to1/x\to\pm\infty\) near \(x=0\). Doesn't match the step boundary data.

name.text: CA30-Q13 Geometric Function Theory — Univalent Functions

questiontext.text: The class \(\mathcal{S}\) consists of univalent (injective) functions \(f(z) = z + \sum_{n=2}^\infty a_n z^n\) on \(\mathbb{D}\). The Koebe one-quarter theorem states \(f(\mathbb{D})\supset\mathbb{D}_{1/4}\). The distortion theorem (Koebe): for \(f\in\mathcal{S}\) and \(|z|=r<1\):

feedback.text: The Koebe distortion theorem gives sharp bounds: \(\frac{r}{(1+r)^2}\leq|f(z)|\leq\frac{r}{(1-r)^2}\) for \(|z|=r\). The bounds are attained by the Koebe function \(k(z)=z/(1-z)^2\) and its rotations. At \(r\to0\): both bounds \(\to r\) (consistent with \(f'(0)=1\)).
These bounds are too tight and are not sharp for the class \(\mathcal{S}\). The correct Koebe bounds involve \((1\pm r)^2\) in the denominator.
This is false; the Koebe function has \(|k(r)| = r/(1-r)^2 > r\) for \(r>0\). Univalent functions can exceed \(r\) in modulus.
The Koebe distortion theorem provides universal (sharp) bounds for all \(f\in\mathcal{S}\). The bounds depend only on \(r=|z|\).

questiontext.text: The Schwarz-Pick lemma states that analytic maps \(f:\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\) are non-expanding in the Poincaré metric. Specifically: \(\frac{|f'(z)|}{1-|f(z)|^2} \leq \frac{1}{1-|z|^2}\). Equality holds for all \(z\) iff \(f\) is an automorphism of \(\mathbb{D}\). For a non-automorphism \(f\) with \(f(0)=0\):

feedback.text: With \(f(0)=0\) and \(f\) non-automorphic, the Schwarz lemma gives \(|f'(0)| \leq 1\) with equality iff \(f(z)=e^{i\theta}z\). For a non-automorphism, we get strict inequality \(|f'(0)|<1\). The function contracts distances in the hyperbolic metric.

answer.text: \(|f'(0)| = 1\) always (derivatives on the boundary are isometries)

feedback.text: At interior points (not on the boundary), the Schwarz lemma gives \(|f'(0)|\leq1\), with equality only for rotations. Non-automorphisms have \(|f'(0)|<1\).
Analytic maps \(\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}\) are contractions (or isometries) in the Poincaré metric. \(|f'(0)|>1\) is impossible for analytic self-maps of \(\mathbb{D}\) by the Schwarz lemma.
The Schwarz lemma provides a sharp constraint: \(|f'(0)|\leq1\) with equality iff \(f(z)=e^{i\theta}z\).

questiontext.text: Liouville's theorem (generalized): if \(f\) is entire and \(|f(z)|\leq M|z|^n\) for all large \(|z|\), then \(f\) is a polynomial of degree \(\leq n\). This follows from Cauchy's inequality applied to \(f^{(n+1)}(0)\). What can we conclude about an entire function \(f\) satisfying \(|f(z)|\leq 5e^{3|z|^2}\)?

answer.text: \(f\) has order at most 2 (but may not be a polynomial)

feedback.text: The bound \(|f|\leq5e^{3r^2}\) shows \(\log M(r)\leq3r^2+\log5\), so \(\rho\leq2\). But \(f\) need not be a polynomial: \(f(z)=e^{z^2}\) satisfies the bound and is not a polynomial. The polynomial conclusion requires polynomial (not exponential) growth.
Only a uniform bound \(|f|\leq M\) (order 0 growth) gives a constant by Liouville. Exponential-of-polynomial growth allows non-constant functions.
The polynomial bound \(|f|\leq M|z|^2\) gives a polynomial. But exponential growth like \(5e^{3r^2}\) allows entire functions of order 2 that are not polynomials.

answer.text: \(f\) must be \(f(z) = ce^{z^2}\) for some constant \(c\)

feedback.text: Many functions satisfy this bound (any entire function of order \(<2\) or order 2 with finite type). The bound does not force a specific form.

name.text: CA30-Q16 Residue Theorem — Evaluation of Improper Integrals

questiontext.text: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\dfrac{\cos(ax)}{1+x^2}\,dx\) for \(a>0\) using residues. Consider \(\oint_{C_R}e^{iaz}/(1+z^2)\,dz\) where \(C_R\) is the upper semicircle. The pole in the upper half-plane is at \(z=i\) with residue \(e^{-a}/(2i)\). The result is:

feedback.text: \(\text{Res}(e^{iaz}/(1+z^2);i) = e^{ia\cdot i}/(i+i) = e^{-a}/(2i)\). Residue theorem: \(\oint_{C_R}=2\pi i\cdot e^{-a}/(2i)=\pi e^{-a}\). By Jordan's lemma, the semicircle contribution vanishes. So \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{iax}/(1+x^2)dx=\pi e^{-a}\). Taking real part: \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty\cos(ax)/(1+x^2)dx=\pi e^{-a}\). Half of this: \(\int_0^\infty = \pi e^{-a}/2\).
This is the full integral \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty\cos(ax)/(1+x^2)dx\). The half-line integral \(\int_0^\infty\) is half of this: \(\pi e^{-a}/2\).
For \(a=0\): \(\int_0^\infty dx/(1+x^2) = \pi/2\) ✓. But for \(a>0\), the result is \(\frac{\pi}{2}e^{-a}\neq\frac{\pi}{2}\).
The integral is non-zero for \(a\geq0\). It equals \(\frac{\pi}{2}e^{-a}>0\).

questiontext.text: Fatou's theorem: if \(f\in H^p(\mathbb{D})\) (Hardy space with \(p\geq1\)), then \(f\) has non-tangential limits almost everywhere on \(\partial\mathbb{D}\). The non-tangential limit (also called the angular limit) at \(e^{i\theta_0}\) exists iff \(f\) converges as \(z\to e^{i\theta_0}\) from within each Stolz angle. This theorem implies:

answer.text: Bounded analytic functions on \(\mathbb{D}\) have boundary values a.e., even if they don't extend continuously

feedback.text: Every \(f\in H^\infty(\mathbb{D})\) (bounded analytic functions) belongs to all \(H^p\) spaces. By Fatou, \(f\) has non-tangential boundary limits a.e. on \(\partial\mathbb{D}\). This recovers boundary data even for functions without continuous extensions (like \(\exp(-(1+z)/(1-z))\) near \(z=1\)).

answer.text: Every analytic function on the disk extends continuously to the closed disk

feedback.text: False. Functions like \(\sin(1/(1-z))\) have no boundary value at \(z=1\). Fatou guarantees a.e. non-tangential limits only for Hardy space functions.

answer.text: The boundary function of an analytic function is always continuous

feedback.text: The boundary function (non-tangential limit) is in \(L^p(\partial\mathbb{D})\) but may be discontinuous. Fatou gives \(L^p\) boundary values, not continuous ones.

answer.text: Analytic functions cannot have essential singularities on the boundary

feedback.text: Analytic functions can have essential singularities at boundary points (e.g., \(e^{1/(z-1)}\) near \(z=1\)). Fatou's theorem concerns Hardy space functions, which have slightly more regularity.

name.text: CA30-Q18 Green's Function and Conformal Mapping

questiontext.text: The Green's function \(G(z,w)\) for a domain \(D\) is the harmonic function in \(D\setminus\{w\}\) with \(G(z,w) = -\log|z-w| + h(z,w)\) (\(h\) harmonic in all of \(D\)) satisfying \(G=0\) on \(\partial D\). For the unit disk, the Green's function is:

feedback.text: The Green's function for \(\mathbb{D}\): \(G(z,w) = -\log|z-w|+\log|1-\bar{w}z| = \log\left|\frac{1-\bar{w}z}{z-w}\right|\). It vanishes when \(|z|=1\): \(|1-\bar{w}z|=|z-w|\) on \(\partial\mathbb{D}\) (since for \(|z|=1\): \(1-\bar{w}z = -z(\bar{z}-\bar{w}) = -z\overline{(z-w)}\), so \(|1-\bar{w}z|=|z-w|\)).
This is the fundamental solution of the Laplacian, but it does NOT vanish on \(\partial\mathbb{D}\). The Green's function requires a correction term to satisfy the zero boundary condition.
This doesn't represent the Green's function for the disk. The correction term uses the reflection principle, giving \(\log|1-\bar{w}z|\) not \(\log|z+w|\).
This is not a harmonic function (the modulus squared of a complex number is not harmonic unless the function is linear).

questiontext.text: An algebraic function is defined by \(P(z,w)=0\) for a polynomial \(P\). The Riemann surface of \(w^2 = (z^2-1)\) has two sheets connected at branch points \(z=\pm1\). The function \(\sqrt{z^2-1}\) is single-valued on \(\mathbb{C}\setminus[-1,1]\) (with appropriate branch cut). On this domain, the two branches are \(w = \pm\sqrt{z^2-1}\). How are they related?

answer.text: They are negatives of each other, and choosing a branch cut \([-1,1]\) makes each branch analytic in \(\mathbb{C}\setminus[-1,1]\)

feedback.text: The two branches \(\pm\sqrt{z^2-1}\) are negatives of each other. With the branch cut along \([-1,1]\), each branch is single-valued and analytic in \(\mathbb{C}\setminus[-1,1]\). The branch cut allows a continuous choice of sign of the square root. Crossing the cut switches between the two branches.

answer.text: They are complex conjugates of each other

feedback.text: The two branches \(w\) and \(-w\) are negatives, not necessarily complex conjugates. They are complex conjugates only if \(w\) is purely imaginary.

answer.text: One branch is analytic and the other is not

feedback.text: Both branches are equally analytic on \(\mathbb{C}\setminus[-1,1]\). The choice of branch cut determines which is the "principal" branch.

At \(z=\pm1\): \(\sqrt{z^2-1}=0\), and both branches give 0. But this is a branch point where the Riemann surface has a ramification point — the two sheets come together, not "coincide as analytic functions."

name.text: CA30-Q20 Prime Number Theorem — Zero-Free Region

questiontext.text: The classical zero-free region for \(\zeta(s)\) is: \(\zeta(s)\neq0\) for \(\sigma = \text{Re}(s) \geq 1 - c/\log(|t|+2)\) (for some constant \(c>0\), where \(s=\sigma+it\)). This asymptotes toward the critical line as \(|t|\to\infty\). The best known zero-free region (up to 2024) due to Korobov-Vinogradov is of the form:

feedback.text: The Korobov-Vinogradov method gives a zero-free region \(\sigma \geq 1 - c/(\log|t|)^{2/3}(\log\log|t|)^{1/3}\), which is slightly wider than the classical region \(1-c/\log|t|\). This region is derived from exponential sum estimates and represents the state of the art for zero-free regions of \(\zeta(s)\).
The Riemann Hypothesis is \(\sigma=1/2\) for all non-trivial zeros, which remains unproven. The known zero-free regions are much closer to \(\sigma=1\).
The Korobov-Vinogradov method improves the exponent from \(1\) to \(2/3\) in the denominator, giving a wider zero-free region than just \(1/\log|t|\).
\(\zeta(s)\) has zeros in \(\sigma>0\) (the non-trivial zeros in the critical strip \(0<\sigma<1\)). The zero-free region is near \(\sigma=1\), not all of \(\sigma>0\).
